Using the calculated values a Simulink model was constructed with the transfer function shown in Equation 2. The voltage input was taken from the experimental data of the lab and fed into the transfer function to produce a simulated velocity output response. Figure 3, showcases the square wave voltage input with values from [-1.5, 1.5] [![](data:image/png;base64,)] respectively. 

From above, immediately the first difference is visible in the noise of the two plots. The measured curve has significant levels of noise that are visible whilst the simulated curve is entirely smooth. Likewise, the measured plot indicates a slight period once the velocity peaks that remains flat before decreasing as a response to a step voltage change. In the simulated model, once the velocity reaches a theoretical maximum it instantaneously begins to as a response to the change in voltage.

To add, the amplitude of the simulated plant is symmetrical where the peaks and troughs of the wave are equidistant from zero. On the measured velocity graph, the portion of waves above zero are slightly higher than those below the zero line. This indicates that potentially there is some directionality involved in the operation of the cart system. It could be that frictional losses in one direction are larger than in the opposing direction or nonlinear damping behavior contributing to an asymmetric waveform.

wn = 18.9
damping = 0.27434
